sessions.title: Agents in the Real World
sessions.set_the_stage:
We open with the practitioner's view. Before we touch any code, we answer the questions a working professional actually asks: what is an agent, where does it help, where does it fall down, and how do we keep it safe.

I'll walk you through these slides one idea at a time, with a wrong way and a right way for each. We'll slow down on the risks and on keeping a human in the loop; that is the grown-up part of this conversation.

By the end, you should be able to say in one sentence what an agent reads, decides, and produces, and name the one place it could quietly go wrong.

sessions.title: The Humanics of AI
sessions.set_the_stage:
This is the thinking layer, and it is where the real skill lives. The best agent-builders are not the fastest typists; they are the clearest thinkers. We'll work through first principles, systems thinking, decomposition, and how to define what good even means.

None of this is abstract. Every habit here points straight at the recruitment agent we build next. Breaking a task into atoms, seeing the whole pipe, and framing a sharp question are exactly what will make that agent easy to design.

Take these seriously and the build becomes the easy part. The clear thinking is what you'll carry into every agent you ever make.

sessions.title: The Mechanics of AI
sessions.set_the_stage:
Now the building blocks, kept deliberately simple: a model (text in, text out), a prompt (your instructions), structured output (neat data, not prose), and a key (just a password).

Then the anatomy every agent shares, input to brain to output, and the one move that makes real agents work: splitting the job into three single-purpose agents that hand clean data down the line.

This is our bridge from idea to build. I'll keep it tight and concrete, and resist the urge to go down any rabbit holes about how models work inside.

build.title: I'll hand out the pack
build.description: You'll each get the 25-resume PDF and the one-page job description. Keep your laptops closed for now.

build.title: Rank by hand, and time it
build.description: Pick your top 5 to interview, with one line of reasoning each. I'll put a 15-minute timer on the screen. Write down how long it actually takes you.

build.title: We'll surface the steps together
build.description: A few of you will walk us through how you did it, and we'll draw the shared steps on the board: read, pull the key facts, compare to the JD, rank, recommend. Those steps become our agent design.

sessions.title: The Recruitment Challenge
sessions.set_the_stage:
Here is your problem: 25 resumes and one job description. Recommend the top 5 to interview. You'll do it by hand first, with no AI, and time yourselves.

This is not busywork. Doing it by hand gives us two things we need later: a real 'before' number to measure the agent against, and a felt sense of the actual steps, read, pull facts, compare to the JD, rank, hand over.

Most of you will not finish all 25 in the time. That is exactly the point. Feel the load, because that is the load the agent is about to lift for you.

build.title: Agent 1, Ingest and Extract
build.description: It takes the resume files and returns clean, structured facts for each candidate: name, years, skills, tools, evidence of impact. This is the contract the next agent depends on.

build.title: Agent 2, Judge and Prioritise
build.description: It takes those facts, scores them against the JD rubric with evidence for each score, applies any hard rules in code, and returns a ranked top 5.

build.title: Agent 3, Communicate
build.description: It takes the top 5 and drafts a clear recommendation to the hiring manager. It drafts; you read and send. Never auto-send.

build.title: We'll draw the contract
build.description: We make the handshake explicit: Agent 1's output shape is exactly Agent 2's input shape. Fix the fields once and the pipeline holds.

sessions.title: Design the Agents (No Code)
sessions.set_the_stage:
Now we turn the board into a design, still with no code. First we'll state the pure logic: ingest the resumes, extract the key information, build a rubric from the JD, compare, prioritise the top 5, and communicate them to the hiring manager.

Then we map that logic onto the agent anatomy and split it into three single-purpose agents. By the end of this session you should be able to draw the whole solution on paper before we ever open Colab.

We'll hold the line on one rule: Agent 3 drafts the note; a human sends it. The agent recommends, you decide.

build.title: Pre-flight
build.description: I'll run the setup cells top to bottom: install, paste key, engine, load the 25 resumes and the JD. Watch for the green confirmations.

build.title: Agent 1 runs
build.description: We extract structured facts from all 25 resumes and watch messy documents become a clean table.

build.title: Agent 2 runs
build.description: We score against the rubric with evidence, apply the hard rule, and reveal the ranked top 5.

build.title: Agent 3 runs
build.description: We draft the recommendation to the hiring manager and read it aloud. We do not send it: that stays your call.

build.title: We validate together
build.description: We lay the agent's 5 next to your by-hand 5 and talk through the overlap and the differences. This is how you learn to trust an agent.

sessions.title: Build It Live
sessions.set_the_stage:
Now I'll build the real thing in front of you, live, in a Google Colab notebook, running it step by step on the actual 25 resumes. I'm using the paid demo key so it never stalls while you're watching.

Watch me narrate every cell back to the design on the board: this cell is Agent 1 extracting, this cell is Agent 2 judging, this one drafts for the human. The code should feel like the obvious transcription of a design you already understand.

At the end we close the loop on trust: we compare the agent's top 5 to the top 5 you picked by hand yesterday. Where they agree, where they differ, and why.

build.title: Pick from the palette
build.description: Choose the use-case closest to your world. Same three agents, different job.

build.title: Change only the marked lines
build.description: You'll swap the documents, the extract fields, the rubric, and the hard rule. Everything else stays exactly as it is.

build.title: Run it on the fallback
build.description: Leave the fallback switch on. It runs on saved results so nothing blocks you. Flip it off only if you want to go live on your own key.

build.title: Defend one choice
build.description: Pair up and justify one design choice to another pair: why that criterion, why that weight, why that hard rule. Building is shallow; defending is deep.

sessions.title: Build Your Own Agent
sessions.set_the_stage:
Now I'll hand over the notebook to you. You'll pick one use-case from the palette, from finops, support, sales, and more, and configure the exact same three-agent design for it. You only change the marked lines.

This run defaults to the fallback switch, so it works with zero friction even if the free tier hiccups. I'll say it plainly: you already learned the live key this morning, so this run is about the pattern, not the plumbing. The live path is one flip away if you want it.

The point here is ownership. When you watch the same machine work on a problem from your own world, that is when it truly clicks.

sessions.title: The Valuenomics of AI
sessions.set_the_stage:
Now we talk value. A working agent is not the prize; the outcome is. We'll trace the chain from data to insight to narrative to influence to outcome, and see how the same shape unlocks value across domains.

Then we'll widen the toolbox beyond the screen-and-rank agent you built, into the other patterns you'll meet out there, each with a place it genuinely wins.

The thread running through all of it: the honest, defensible story you can tell a recruiter, built on a real agent and a real number you measured yourself.

build.title: Name your next agent
build.description: Pick one boring, repeated, judgement-based task in your own life and commit to building an agent for it this week.

build.title: Start your portfolio
build.description: Save every agent you build, each with its before-and-after number. That folder is your proof, and it grows from today.

sessions.title: What Next
sessions.set_the_stage:
We close on the road ahead. You walked in curious and you leave able to build; this session is about what you do with that.

We'll talk about how to spot opportunities, how to keep building, how to turn this into something real, placements, freelance, even a venture, and the mindset that outlasts any single tool.

End it on one concrete commitment: the one agent each of you will build this week. Momentum from here is everything.

ideas.name: Recruitment Screener
ideas.for_whom: You, if HR or campus placements is your world
ideas.problem: A recruiter drowns in 200 resumes per role and skims the first 20 by gut feel.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts facts from every resume, scores them against the JD rubric with evidence, and returns a ranked shortlist to interview.

ideas.name: Invoice Triage
ideas.for_whom: You, if finance or FinOps is your world
ideas.problem: Accounts payable processes hundreds of invoices; the risky ones hide in the pile.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts each invoice's key fields, flags anything needing human approval by a hard rule, and prioritises the rest for payment.

ideas.name: Support Ticket Triage
ideas.for_whom: You, if customer support is your world
ideas.problem: A support queue mixes trivial and urgent; the loudest ticket is rarely the most important.
ideas.what_app_does: Reads each ticket, scores impact and urgency with evidence, and auto-escalates anything touching money or legal.

ideas.name: Lead Scorer
ideas.for_whom: You, if sales or growth is your world
ideas.problem: Reps chase every inbound lead equally and waste the week on poor-fit ones.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts each lead's signals, scores fit against your ideal-customer rubric, and ranks who to call first.

ideas.name: Expense Claim Checker
ideas.for_whom: You, if finance or audit is your world
ideas.problem: Expense claims are rubber-stamped or randomly sampled; policy breaches slip through.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts each claim's details, flags policy violations by hard rule, and ranks the rest for a quick human review.

ideas.name: Incident Report Triage
ideas.for_whom: You, if operations or safety is your world
ideas.problem: Incident reports pile up and the serious ones are not read first.
ideas.what_app_does: Reads each report, scores severity with evidence, and pushes the critical ones to the top with a reason.

ideas.name: Application Screener
ideas.for_whom: You, if admissions or programs is your world
ideas.problem: A program gets far more applications than it can read carefully.
ideas.what_app_does: Extracts each application's facts, scores them against the selection rubric with evidence, and returns a shortlist.

ideas.name: Feedback Prioritiser
ideas.for_whom: You, if product or CX is your world
ideas.problem: Hundreds of reviews and survey verbatims arrive; the churn-risk signals are buried.
ideas.what_app_does: Reads each piece of feedback, scores it by churn risk and theme, and ranks what to act on this week.
